title: Splitting & Joining ✂️
description: Break strings apart and stitch them together.

slides.title: split() — Break Apart ✂️
slides.content:
# Splitting Strings

`split()` breaks a string into a **list of substrings**:

```
"Hello World".split()         # ["Hello", "World"]
"a,b,c,d".split(",")          # ["a", "b", "c", "d"]
"2024-01-15".split("-")       # ["2024", "01", "15"]
```

By default, `split()` splits on **whitespace** (spaces, tabs, newlines). You can specify any separator!

This is incredibly useful for **parsing data** — CSV files, user input, URLs, and more.

slides.title: join() — Stitch Together 🧵
slides.content:
## The Opposite of split()

`join()` combines a **list of strings** into one string:

```
words = ["Hello", "World"]
" ".join(words)           # "Hello World"

letters = ["P", "y", "t", "h", "o", "n"]
"".join(letters)          # "Python"

items = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
", ".join(items)          # "apple, banana, cherry"
```

**Syntax:** `separator.join(list_of_strings)`

The separator goes between each element.

slides.title: Split & Join 🎬
slides.content: Practical examples of splitting and joining:
slides.codeSnippet:
# Split a sentence into words
sentence = "Python is really awesome"
words = sentence.split()
print("Words:", words)
print("Count:", len(words))

# Join with different separators
print(" → ".join(words))
print(" | ".join(words))

# CSV parsing
csv_line = "Alice,25,Engineer"
fields = csv_line.split(",")
print(f"Name: {fields[0]}, Age: {fields[1]}")

quizData.question: What does "a-b-c".split("-") return?
quizData.explanation: split('-') breaks the string at each '-', producing a list of substrings.

quizData.question: What does "-".join(["a","b","c"]) return?
quizData.explanation: join() inserts the separator between elements: a-b-c.
